sentences.text: Two months-- good two months in time.
The only thing I have to tell you that everything what we are talking-- all of us, we will never recall dates, days.
I didn't know from a Monday, or a Tuesday, or on Shabbos.
We lived like Adam and Eve, you know, wilderness.
Our lives were numbers, that's all.
So good-- over two months until I came into the ghetto.
How did you eat?

Very hard to tell you-- anything that came into my hands-- out of the fields, potatoes left out in the fields, dug out, could be trees, anything-- to take away a bowl of soup from a dog, to kill a chicken if I could get it, anything to survive-- worse than that.
Wherever I could-- in the fields.
It was cold.
I snuck myself into places.
I slept through-- lucky the time was when it went into the springtime to the fall-- I mean into the summertime.
So I could survive a little.
By myself.
All what I did it by myself.
Yes-- Russian Jews, plenty of Jewish people from Poland arrested by the Russians, given sentences.
Cold and bitter.
Yes, I worked.
I worked outside.
We used to go out in 50 degree below, 60 degree below.
[SIGHS] And-- wasn't such-- such a good-- they are not good stories to relay, but we have to do it, just to remind ourselves.
In Russia, you couldn't get sick.
If you broke a leg, it was no excuse.
You have to have fever or die.
If you didn't have any fever, you were not excused from going out to work.
You could have an earache, headache, toothache-- you had to have fever or kill yourself.
Siberia, in the camps--
In a concentration camp.
We were under guards, Russian guards.
In Siberia, in Russian, you used to call comrade, you know, under the communist regime.
But when you went into a prison or to a camp, the guard wasn't a comrade anymore.
He was not a friend.
He was a citizen.
You had to call him citizen.
And by mistake, once I called a guard comrade.
He hit me with his rifle.
I'm not your comrade.
No kind of wage-- we worked for a piece of bread.
We had to work to get a limited amount of bread, 90 decagrams, which was like two pounds.
We had to have good marks working that we the day good to get the 90 decagram of bread for the day.
If not, you could have gotten about four ounces of bread a day.
It depends.
And the soup-- they gave us soup.
It wasn't too good, you know.
It was in a concentration camp.
We came home frozen.
We didn't have any blankets.
I slipped down my pants over my legs.
I took off my wet coat from all day and put a sleeve behind my ear as a pillow and covered myself with the other half.
And at 4 o'clock in the morning, you were awakened to go to work again.
You had to go out on a-- what did I call that-- like the army goes out in the morning on the--
Right, to stand them in line.
And they crowded us.
If we all there, we will march to work-- and the guards with dogs.
Yeah, well, listen, I made friends.
Russian people most had friends.
If I found a Jew, didn't mean much.
Because if a Russian Jew, they all went like we are.
Stalin-- he had a Jewish wife, but he didn't like the Jews.
Where?
To ghetto.
I escaped from Siberia back to Poland, into ghetto.
Rowne.
And when I escaped the ghetto, I went into the forest, to the underground.
I was in the ghetto not too long.
It didn't last very long.
It was a short time-- a month, six weeks, and over it.
The ghetto was liquidated very shortly.
Because in those places, the end to 1942, the beginning of '43, they already had Auschwitz built.
And when I went into the underground, until 1943-- I got out in the middle, close to the middle-- we never knew about the atrocities, what happened.
At that time, Auschwitz begin to function, Birkenau, the gas chambers.
Until then, they sent people to labor camps-- the Germans, too.
But they built Auschwitz through time.
And I escaped in the right time.
But the people from those places over there, they didn't want to transport them into Treblinka, or to Auschwitz, or  wherever.
That they liquidated-- they liquidated the ghettos, yes.
No.
I wasn't there.
But I have one friend survived.
He's in Israel.
One friend I met in Philadelphia down the years that he was Rowne in the ghetto.
And I didn't know him in the ghetto.
But we talk, you know.
We get together.
Went into the forest.
This was in the end of '42.
In the forest, we were-- when I escaped, we formed.
We had a couple Jewish people with me, a couple Jewish men.
It was one woman.
She was Polish with a Jewish man.
And we were about eight people, nine people.
We built bunkers in the forest-- hiding.
We won't get strong enough or equipped enough to go and to resist until we enlarged ourselves.
Due time, a lot of Russian prisoners taken by the Germans, they start to escape, too.
For the Germans, it was a curse, the weather in Russia.
The weather was a curse for them, because the cold weather-- they couldn't take as much as the Russians.
And a lot of Russian had a chance to escape from the Germans at due time, when they took them in as prisoners.
So one of a Russian captain-- Polish Russian captain, army captain- escaped.
And we got to him.
And we took him in with us.
And at due time, we were a little army of 36 people, 35.
And then we began to attack German headquarters to go out and fight for food.
And soon we got a little bit ammunition in our hands.
We couldn't get any machine guns.
We couldn't get any tanks.
But we equipped ourselves with rifles, guns.
We attacked German headquarters.
And then we went out, and we destroyed transports, bridges, you know, demolished tanks to stop the Germans from proceeding to the front lines.
Well, that's what I said.
We attacked German headquarters.
We had to do things-- all day, oh, you are?
Oh, that's me.
And that's the way we went to get food.
We went out to a village to get food and bring back to the underground.
So we had to make a-- a way how to get it.
So we would knock at the door.
So we couldn't shoot in the middle of the night.
It echos.
So we handled ourselves any other ways.
Oh, you give what we want, or we'll take it.
So some of them were very, you know, cooperative.
And they took us, and they gave us what we need.
We were afraid, you know, they shouldn't notify the Germans.
But those years, they didn't have any telephones like we have in here to pick up a telephone and call somebody.
So we were lucky one way.
But we still couldn't trust with whom we're dealing.
But some of them cooperated.
They were afraid for their lives.
And we were afraid of the Ukrainians.
The Ukrainians, and the Lithuanians, and the Latvians, and the Polish-- they were our enemies-- the biggest.
That's why Hitler built all those crematoriums on the Polish territory, That they were the biggest help.
If you read now in the newspaper, every day you see somebody else is denaturalized from this country, taken away the citizenship.
And if you read the name, it's a Polack, or a Ukrainian, or a Russian.
You're not finding a German.
But you find all the other people.
They were the help to destroy us.
I stayed in the forest until close to the end of '43, when the Russians start to get the upper hand in the war.
Then when they took over territory, they reached where we were probably about three times on the Russian hands and on the German hands.
But we didn't want to get out from the underground.
We were unsure what-- in whose hands we'll fall in yet.
And I didn't want to fall back in the Russian hands, because I escaped from Siberia.
So I was like in the middle of nowhere.
I had to only live with luck, with Gold's help, to drive, and survive, and fight through.
You see, it was a big difference.
We had three survivors-- three kind of survivors in this war-- people who went to concentration camp, destiny was dead.
By luck, they survived.
People were hidden on Aryan papers, you know?
Also by luck that, as long that the other nationality-- the Poles or whoever kept them wants to keep them, and not to sell them out to the Germans.
And we played the different luck.
We had to play for survival.
Oh you, oh, it's me.
No.
They knew I'm a Jew.
I was always a Jew.
But I didn't-- couldn't open up enough.
I couldn't open up enough.
Because I didn't have enough support.
I was by myself.
I went to sleep, and who knew if somebody could come over and kill me, whoever it was.
No, no.
We had a mixture.
We had Poles.
We had Poles, a few Poles.
And we  had a couple Russians-- more Russians.
But the captain--
Not Jewish.
The captain was a big help to us.
I had another two Jewish people.
One man had a Polish woman.
But she was during the war, she got with him together.
And finally, they went up in the underground.
There was no difference at that time.
I was the same hero as the other fellow.
And he was the same hero as I am.
We only went for one cause-- to survive and to destroy the Germans as much as we could.
I went out out of the forest.
I head back to Poland.
And I wind up in Lublin.
And I went into the camp on Majdanek-- which they formed a Polish army-- army on the communists.
All had the Polish army-- Polish uniforms, but Russian officers.
But I didn't want to give my identity that I was in Siberia.
I wind up in the underground-- I didn't want to come back in their hands.
And I enlisted myself in the Polish army in Lublin, in Majdanek, where the prisoners-- concentration prisoners slept on the same bunks we were sleeping as army recruits-- same bunks.
And I was six or eight weeks in a school anti-aircraft.
And my education-- what I was in Europe, educated at least public school-- helped me a lot.
And then I spoke already Russian fluently, Polish.
And I enlisted in the army.
And a matter of fact, when we came in the first line, I had to read the map in German for the Russian officers.
I had to guide them.
I was sitting in a Jeep with them and reading.
And I find myself, all in, better.
But I was fighting not to get any ranks in the army.
I didn't want to accept.
I didn't want to stay in.
Whatever it would happen if I survived the end to the war, I didn't want to stay in Poland.
I had dreams that I was going to be left in Poland.
I had dreams to hope that I find my family, that I come home.
I didn't-- we didn't know until 1943 in the underground, until we liquidated German radios, equipment, you know, to communicate-- and we start to communicate, not out.
But we start to listen to the outside world.
We had a man, he understood a little English-- not too much.
But we could catch up slowly what's going on outside, what Hitler's doing already.
I was anti-aircraft, shooting down planes, Messerschmitts.
I had to go to school for two months in Lublin-- be in school for it.
And then they sent me right away to the first line.
Until the end of the war, until 1945.
It was over a year.
I wasn't long in the army-- over a year.
I-- I did-- made certain ways to get discharged.
I have my book in here, being discharged-- I'll show it to you-- from the army.
Honorable discharge.
I didn't want to escape.
I didn't want to be caught as a deserter.
Because I knew, if ever the time will come and I'll come back ever to hometown, I won't live in Poland anymore.
Being in the army, not too much of it.
I always looked for a synagogue wherever I came into a Polish town.
I came in and being in the army, in a town they called Miedzyzdroje.
And I was in the Polish uniform, and I went into town.
And I found pages from Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], you know, Jewish religious articles, books, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I remember me and another soldier took it to the-- being-- took to the cemetery where it was.
And we digged a grave.
And we buried all those things, covered it up.
Because by Jewish people, you're not allowed-- you were not allowed to burn an old [NON-ENGLISH].
You have to do a ritual burying, like we bury a deceased person.
I was in the uniform.
I found them in the street scattered by a-- by a burned shul-- burned synagogue.
So all that was scattered, I put them together as much as I could with a friend of mine.
And we took them to the cemetery.
Which the cemetery was segregated already.
Half of the tombstones were turned over.
You know, the Germans didn't segregate it so much the cemeteries like the Poles did.
I'll come to it.
And I explain to you what happened in my hometown.
The end of '43, '44-- going into '44 in the army.
I'm not ready yet until the war ended.
The war ended May the 8th.
So they took us off already from the first line.
And that settled us into army camps.
And I was sent to a camp near Posen, town of Leshno in Poland.
And we were in camp.
And all right, it's settled down.
And I was thinking for fall off to go home to Bendin, to my hometown, to see if I have left anybody.
So I got fall off 10 days.
I came home.
I had already a cousin coming back from Russia, which he escaped to Russia also.
But he wasn't with me.
One cousin-- we were separated in Russia.
He was in the army with me by coincidence.
When they send me out to the first line, they send him from somewhere else.
And we got together.
And we were one company.
And one cousin was still in the army in Kielce.
And I came home.
I found that cousin alive and living in Bendin.
And then I found my wife now coming home from camp with her sister Molly.
In Bendin, in Bendin.
We had an uncle living in Bendin.
And he's not alive anymore.
He was in Poland.
He didn't want to leave Poland.
Drexler.
Marion.
In Polish, they called him Marion.
But in the Yiddish name was Malach.
Malach is like, I say, a kink in translation in Hebrew-- Malach.
And I found the two sisters.
And they related to me.
My wife is a cousin of mine.
And I told them, I didn't know that we're going to be together.
But I told them I'd like to take them out of Poland.
In which time, I'm six years older than Annie.
So she depended upon that I will.
So I took the two sisters out of Poland.
And I saved my cousin, one in the army.
I went in to the-- bought a civilian suit with a hat, with a coat.
I took him out from the army camp.
I had already tickets.
And Annie and her sister were already in Kattowitz, away from Bendin, awaiting me.
We had already planned out.
I was a big planner and a risk in my life-- how to do it.
And we escaped.
And here we come on the Czechoslovakian Polish border.
We came into Kattowitz.
And from Kattowitz, we got together to go.
We're coming on the border.
Here the Russians again-- passports.
You know, the Russians were controlling Europe all the years.
Everything was under Russian control.
So I speak Russian.
We left all what we took.
Our belongings were left on the train.
We figure, we'll come back.
And I took the two girls.
And I bought a Russian jacket with a Russian hat.
And I said to the Russians, where can I get in here some tea?
And I speak Russian.
He says, go over there to the station.
I said to the girls, in Russian, let's go.
So we run around.
As soon the control passed my wagon, we went back into the train the other way.
And all our belongings were gone.
They stole everything from us on the train.
We didn't have-- if we would walk with all the belongings, they would stopped us.
So I speak Russian. they didn't know.
So we went, and we came into Bratislava in Czechoslovakia to the town.
And we didn't have anything to wear, nothing to change.
Everything what we wore was, We have two ladies, too.
Molly was-- Annie was 19-- not even 19.
And Molly was not even 17.
And I got to take care.
But Jewish people are very helpful.
There was already a committee in Bratislava which they helped all the refugees coming in.
They served soup.
And they located us where to stay.
And I think I made a little mention-- I mentioned to you before that communication after the war was on the wall.
Everything was written on the wall-- names.
I'm going there.
And I went over there.
And this and this name is in here.
And we start to follow.
So we found out that we're going to leave Czechoslovakia.
We're going to Germany.
Well them we did tricky.
I had my cousin, the one I took him out from the army, and the two sisters.
Now we had to cross another border-- illegal, Germans, the Americans on one side, Russian and Czechs on one side.
So we were coming to the border.
I said to my cousin, you go first that the girls shouldn't be left alone.
Always should be somebody to overlook.
Men is different.
I sent him first.
I send Molly next across.
Then Annie went.
And I was the last one.
And we crossed.
So we start to walk away from the border.
